answer.rationale: The single direct New Testament statement of procession is John 15:26, where Jesus names the Spirit as ho para tou patros ekporeuetai (G1607 ekporeuomai, present indicative, paired with the preposition para G3844 plus the genitive tou patros). The verb describes ongoing origination of the Spirit from the Father. John 14:26 reinforces with the Father pempo (G3992, future) the Paraclete in the Son's name. John 14:16 has the Son ask the Father, and the Father gives another Paraclete (allos parakletos, G243 G3875), keeping the Father as the origin in the Son's intercession. John 16:13-15 has the Spirit speaking what he hears (akouo G191), grounding the Spirit's economic mission in receiving from the Father (and from the Son, through whom all the Father has belongs to the Spirit). Procession from the Father is therefore canonically anchored. The classical confession is the Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed (381): to pneuma to hagion ... to ek tou patros ekporeuomenon. All historic Trinitarian lineages affirm the procession from the Father; the inner-Christian dispute is whether to add 'and from the Son' (the filioque, which is treated under doc-spirit-filioque).
answer.notes: Procession from the Father is universally affirmed in classical Trinitarian Christianity: Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, Lutheran, Anglican, Reformed, Methodist, Anabaptist, and Pentecostal lineages all confess it. The inner-Christian dispute, codified at the Council of Toledo (589) for the Latin West and resisted by the Greek East, is whether the Spirit also proceeds from the Son (the filioque), which is its own separate question (doc-spirit-filioque). The cult-marker bar (canonical demonstration plus would-die-for) is NOT met for this specific proposition because the only true denial here would be denial that the Spirit has any eternal origin or distinct hypostatic existence at all, which collapses into denial of the Spirit's personhood or denial of the Trinity. Those broader denials are already cult-marked under doc-trinity, doc-spirit-personhood, and doc-spirit-full-deity. Public-record carriers of denials of the Spirit's distinct existence (and therefore of any procession): Modalists (Sabellians; Oneness Pentecostals like the United Pentecostal Church and Jesus' Name movements; Iglesia ni Cristo on Spirit-as-impersonal-power), Jehovah's Witnesses (Spirit as impersonal active force, not a person who could proceed), Christadelphians (same view, Spirit as God's power), Unitarian Universalists (no Trinitarian commitments). The fruit-vs-precision nuance: most lay Christians recite the Nicene Creed without parsing the word 'proceeds'; they confess what their churches confess, and the canonical anchor (John 15:26) is the one Scripture passage that any classical Trinitarian can name on this point. Confidence is high on procession-from-the-Father; the filioque question is a separate confidence call.

stem_audit.notes: The statement 'The Holy Spirit eternally proceeds from the Father (Nicene-Constantinopolitan confession of 381)' uses confessional language but is a near-verbatim translation of the John 15:26 phrase ho para tou patros ekporeuetai. The verb ekporeuomai G1607 is the canonical lexical anchor; the Council of 381 picked up the participle ekporeuomenon directly from the Johannine text. The statement therefore restates Scripture, not a Reformed confessional gloss. No neutralization needed.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: the Holy Spirit eternally has his origin in the Father. The Bible says this in plain words at John 15:26, where Jesus calls the Spirit the one 'who proceeds from the Father.' The Greek verb behind the word 'proceeds' is in the present tense, which suggests an ongoing, eternal relationship and not a one-time event in history. John 14:16 backs this up. Jesus says he will ask the Father, and the Father will give another Helper. John 14:26 adds that the Father will send the Spirit in Jesus's name. The Father is consistently named as the source from whom the Spirit comes. The strongest complicating texts inside the Bible are the verses where the Son also sends the Spirit. John 15:26 itself says, 'whom I will send to you from the Father,' so the Son is involved in the sending. John 16:7 has Jesus say, 'If I go, I will send him to you.' John 20:22 has the risen Jesus breathe on the disciples and say, 'Receive the Holy Spirit.' Critics, especially in the Eastern Orthodox tradition, point out that 'sending in time' and 'eternally proceeding' are different things. The Father is the eternal origin, while both the Father and the Son act together to send the Spirit into the world at Pentecost. The Western Latin tradition reads the same texts and adds that the Spirit also eternally proceeds from the Son. That added clause is called the filioque and is a different question. On the original 381 confession that the Spirit proceeds from the Father, the Bible's witness is clear and every classical Trinitarian Christian tradition agrees.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Agreement on the Spirit proceeding from the Father is universal across every historic Christian tradition. The Council of Constantinople in 381 wrote the line 'who proceeds from the Father' into the Nicene Creed using the exact verb from John 15:26. The Eastern Orthodox Church holds this confession in its original form and emphasizes what theologians call the monarchy of the Father, meaning the Father is the single source within the Trinity. The Orthodox Church in America states that the Spirit 'proceeds from the Father in eternity in a divinely instantaneous and perpetual movement.' The Roman Catholic Church and the Western Protestant traditions (Lutheran, Anglican, Reformed, Methodist, Anabaptist, Pentecostal) all confess procession from the Father; they additionally add 'and from the Son' (the filioque), but that addition does not deny procession from the Father. The Catholic Catechism teaches that the Father is the first origin of the Spirit and the principle without principle. The Anglican 39 Articles, the Methodist Articles of Religion, the Lutheran Augsburg Confession, the Westminster Confession, and the Belgic Confession all begin from the same 381 confession. The Assemblies of God and other Pentecostal bodies likewise confess procession from the Father. Public-record groups that deny the Spirit's distinct existence and therefore deny any procession include Jehovah's Witnesses (Spirit as impersonal active force), Christadelphians (Spirit as God's power, not a person), Unitarian Universalists (no Trinitarian commitments), and Oneness Pentecostals (Spirit as a mode of the one person, not a distinct hypostasis to proceed). Those denials are downstream of denying the Trinity or the Spirit's personhood; they are addressed under those separate questions.

scripture.key_term: ἐκπορεύομαι ekporeuomai G1607 (proceeds, present indicative middle); παρά para G3844 plus genitive (from beside, from); παράκλητος parakletos G3875; πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας pneuma tes aletheias G4151 G225
scripture.force: Hotan elthe ho parakletos hon ego pempso hymin para tou patros, to pneuma tes aletheias ho para tou patros ekporeuetai, ekeinos martyresei peri emou. Two prepositional phrases with para tou patros bracket the verbal action. The Son sends (pempso, future indicative active) the Helper para tou patros (from the Father's side), and the Spirit proceeds (ekporeuetai, present indicative) para tou patros (from the Father's side). The present tense of ekporeuomai is the lexical hook the Constantinople council used in 381 (ekporeuomenon, present participle) to anchor eternal procession. The verb governs the preposition with the genitive case naming the Father as the origin.

scripture.key_term: πέμπω pempo G3992 (will send, future indicative active); παράκλητος parakletos G3875; πατήρ pater G3962; ὄνομα onoma G3686 (in my name)
scripture.force: Ho de parakletos, to pneuma to hagion, ho pempsei ho pater en to onomati mou, ekeinos hymas didaxei panta. The Father is the grammatical subject of pempsei (will send); the Son's name is the instrumental sphere (en to onomati mou). The Father is named as the sender, consistent with the Father as origin of the Spirit. Note ekeinos (masculine demonstrative) used for the Spirit despite neuter gender of pneuma, indicating personal reference.

scripture.key_term: ἐρωτάω erotao G2065 (ask); δίδωμι didomi G1325 (give); ἄλλος allos G243 (another of the same kind); παράκλητος parakletos G3875
scripture.force: Kago erotoeso ton patera, kai allon parakleton dosei hymin. The Son asks; the Father gives. The Spirit is allos parakletos (another Helper of the same kind as Jesus the first Helper), and the Father is the agent of giving. The chain (Son asks, Father gives) keeps the Father as the source from whom the Spirit comes.

scripture.key_term: πέμπω pempo G3992 (will send); παράκλητος parakletos G3875; ἐάν ean G1437 (if) plus subjunctive (poreuthō)
scripture.force: Ean gar me apeltho, ho parakletos ouk eleusetai pros hymas; ean de poreuthō, pempso auton pros hymas. The Son here is the subject of pempso (I will send). This is a temporal sending into the world, an economic mission. Read alongside John 15:26 (the Father is the eternal origin) and John 14:26 (the Father will send), it shows that both Father and Son act in the temporal sending of the Spirit, while the Father remains the named source of procession. Complicates a strict 'Father alone sends' reading at the temporal level, but does not displace the Father as eternal origin.

scripture.key_term: ὁδηγέω hodegeo G3594 (guide); ἀκούω akouo G191 (hear); λαμβάνω lambano G2983 (receive); ἀναγγέλλω anangello G312 (declare)
scripture.force: Hotan de elthe ekeinos, to pneuma tes aletheias, hodegesei hymas en te aletheia pase. Ou gar lalesei aph heautou, all hosa akousei lalesei. Ekeinos eme doxasei, hoti ek tou emou lempsetai kai anangelei hymin. Panta hosa echei ho pater ema estin. The Spirit speaks what he hears (an act of derivation), declares what he receives (lambano) from the Son, and the Son shares what the Father has. The Spirit's communication is grounded in the Father, mediated through the Son. Supports procession from the Father; complicates a purely monad-from-Father reading because the Spirit lambano (receives) ek tou emou (from the Son's possession).

scripture.key_term: ἐμφυσάω emphysao G1720 (breathe on, only NT occurrence, echoes Gen 2:7 LXX); λαμβάνω lambano G2983 (receive); πνεῦμα ἅγιον G4151 G40
scripture.force: Touto eipon enephysesen kai legei autois, Labete pneuma hagion. The risen Jesus breathes (emphysao, the same verb used in the LXX for God breathing the breath of life into Adam in Gen 2:7) and gives the Spirit. Functional Christological act in the temporal mission. Complicates a Father-only sending reading at the temporal level, while leaving the Father's role as eternal origin (John 15:26) untouched.

scripture.key_term: δεξιά dexia G1188 (right hand); ὑψόω hypsoo G5312 (exalt); ἐπαγγελία epangelia G1860 (promise); ἐκχέω ekcheo G1632 (pour out)
scripture.force: Te dexia oun tou theou hypsotheis, ten te epangelian tou pneumatos tou hagiou labon para tou patros, execheen touto. Peter's Pentecost sermon: the exalted Son received (labon) the promise of the Holy Spirit para tou patros (from the Father). The same prepositional phrase para tou patros used in John 15:26 reappears here for the source. The Son receives from the Father and pours out on the church. Confirms the Father as origin in both the eternal and the economic dimensions.

scripture.key_term: πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας pneuma tes aletheias G4151 G225; παρά para G3844; μένω meno G3306 (abide); ἐν en G1722 (in)
scripture.force: To pneuma tes aletheias, ho ho kosmos ou dynatai labein, hoti ou theorei auto oude ginoskei. Hymeis ginoskete auto, hoti par hymin menei kai en hymin estai. The Spirit is named Spirit of truth; the Spirit abides para (alongside) believers and will be en (in) them. This is the inverse of the procession para tou patros: just as the Spirit comes para tou patros, the Spirit comes to abide para hymin (with them). Lexical pairing inside the same Upper Room Discourse.

scripture.key_term: πνεῦμα θεοῦ pneuma theou G4151 G2316 (Spirit of God); πνεῦμα Χριστοῦ pneuma Christou G4151 G5547 (Spirit of Christ); οἰκέω oikeo G3611 (dwell)
scripture.force: Hymeis de ouk este en sarki alla en pneumati, eiper pneuma theou oikei en hymin. Ei de tis pneuma Christou ouk echei, houtos ouk estin autou. The Spirit is named both Spirit of God (the Father) and Spirit of Christ (the Son). Critics who read procession-from-Father-alone strictly must reckon with pneuma Christou; defenders of single procession from the Father read pneuma Christou as describing temporal mission and personal relation, not eternal origin. Complicates the simpler reading; the canonical pattern names the Spirit as the Spirit of both Father and Son.

scripture.key_term: ἐξαποστέλλω exapostello G1821 (send forth); πνεῦμα τοῦ υἱοῦ pneuma tou huiou G4151 G5207 (Spirit of his Son); καρδία kardia G2588 (heart); κράζω krazo G2896 (cry)
scripture.force: Hoti de este huioi, exapesteilen ho theos to pneuma tou huiou autou eis tas kardias hymon, krazon, Abba ho pater. God (the Father) sends the Spirit of his Son into hearts. The Spirit is named pneuma tou huiou. The Father is the sender; the Son is the one whose Spirit is sent. Complicates a strict Father-alone reading because the Spirit's relation to the Son is named in the very phrase 'Spirit of his Son'.

scripture.key_term: ποταμός potamos G4215 (river); ζωή zoe G2222 (life); ἐκπορεύομαι ekporeuomai G1607 (proceeds); θρόνος thronos G2362 (throne); ἀρνίον arnion G721 (Lamb)
scripture.force: Edeiksen moi potamon hydatos zoes lampron hos krystallon, ekporeuomenon ek tou thronou tou theou kai tou arniou. The verb ekporeuomai (the same verb as John 15:26) describes a river of the water of life proceeding from the throne of God and of the Lamb. Many readers (Catholic, Lutheran, Anglican, Reformed, Methodist) read the river as a figure of the Spirit and as canonical support for the filioque (procession from both Father and Son). Orthodox readers distinguish symbolic procession of life-giving water in apocalyptic vision from hypostatic procession of the divine person. Complicates the question of whether procession from the Father alone is canonically demonstrated, but does not negate that the Father is named as the source in John 15:26.

scripture.key_term: רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים ruach elohim H7307 H430 (Spirit of God); רָחַף rachaph H7363 (hover, brood); עַל al H5921 (over); פָּנֶה panim H6440 (face)
scripture.force: Veruach elohim merachefet al pene hammayim. The Spirit of God hovers over the waters. The Spirit is named as the Spirit OF God (genitive of source), tied to God (here unnamed as Father, but elohim is the broader name covering the Father) from the canon's opening. The Hebrew construction names the Spirit as the Spirit belonging to God, consistent with the New Testament's later disclosure that the Spirit eternally proceeds from the Father.

hermeneutics.notes: Grammatico-historical reading of John 15:26 settles the verdict on procession from the Father. The verb ekporeuomai G1607 with para tou patros lexically names the Father as the source from which the Spirit proceeds. The 381 council picked the same verb and prepositional phrase verbatim. No interpretive framework (covenant, dispensational, new-covenant, progressive-covenantal) produces a different verdict on procession from the Father; this sits prior to every framework. Analogia scripturae is invoked because the Father is named as the sender of the Spirit at John 14:26, the giver of the Spirit at John 14:16, the one from whom the exalted Son receives the Spirit at Acts 2:33, and the source of the Spirit named the Spirit of God throughout the canon (Gen 1:2, Rom 8:9, 1 Cor 2:11). Progressive revelation is in play: the Old Testament names the Spirit as the Spirit OF God or the Spirit OF the LORD (Gen 1:2, Judg 3:10, Ps 51:11, Isa 11:2, Isa 61:1, Ezek 36:27, Joel 2:28); the New Testament discloses the Father as the specific person within the one God from whom the Spirit eternally proceeds. The separate question of whether the Spirit also proceeds from the Son (the filioque) is handled at doc-spirit-filioque; on the original 381 confession of procession from the Father, no interpretive lens produces a different verdict. Competing-lens verdicts: none on the Father-procession question itself.

counter_witness.anchor: Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed (381), the article on the Holy Spirit
counter_witness.key_phrase: And in the Holy Spirit, the Lord, the Giver of life, who proceeds from the Father (to ek tou patros ekporeuomenon), who with the Father and the Son together is worshipped and glorified.

counter_witness.anchor: Athanasius, Letters to Serapion on the Holy Spirit, c. AD 359; CCEL NPNF Series 2 Vol 4
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Spirit is from the Father, not as a creature made out of nothing but as belonging to the Father's own being and proceeding from him as proper to the Godhead.

counter_witness.anchor: Gregory of Nazianzus, Fifth Theological Oration (Oration 31), On the Holy Spirit, c. AD 380; CCEL NPNF Series 2 Vol 7
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Spirit is neither begotten nor unbegotten; he proceeds (ekporeuetai) from the Father, having a proper hypostasis distinct from the Son's generation.

counter_witness.anchor: Photius of Constantinople, Mystagogy of the Holy Spirit, c. AD 885 (the Eastern critique of filioque)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father alone (ek monou tou patros) as the eternal cause; the Father is the single source within the Trinity, preserving the monarchy of the Father.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church, paragraphs 245-248 on the procession of the Holy Spirit
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Father is the first origin and the principle without principle of the Spirit; the Western tradition adds that the Spirit also proceeds from the Son as from one principle through two persons.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, Doctrine: The Holy Spirit and the Procession
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father in eternity in a divinely instantaneous and perpetual movement; the Father is the eternal origin and source of the Spirit.

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession (1530), Article I, Of God; bookofconcord.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: Three Persons, of the same essence and power, who also are coeternal, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost; the Spirit proceeding eternally from the Father (and the Son).

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion (1571), Article V, Of the Holy Ghost
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Ghost, proceeding from the Father and the Son, is of one substance, majesty, and glory with the Father and the Son, very and eternal God.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith (1646), Chapter 2, paragraph 3
counter_witness.key_phrase: In the unity of the Godhead there be three Persons of one substance, power, and eternity: God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Ghost; the Holy Ghost eternally proceeding from the Father and the Son.

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles), Article IV, Of the Holy Ghost; umc.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Ghost, proceeding from the Father and the Son, is of one substance, majesty, and glory with the Father and the Son, very and eternal God.

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession of Faith (1632), Article I, Of God
counter_witness.key_phrase: We believe in one eternal, almighty, and incomprehensible God, Father, Son, and Holy Ghost; in three persons coequal, with the Spirit proceeding from the Father.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, Statement of Fundamental Truths, Article 2 (The Adorable Godhead); ag.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: The one true God has revealed Himself as Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, three persons in the one Godhead, with the Spirit eternally proceeding from the Father.

web.quote: John 15:26: to pneuma tes aletheias ho para tou patros ekporeuetai. Verb ekporeuomai G1607 present indicative, with para G3844 plus genitive tou patros (from the Father).
John 14:26: ho pempsei ho pater en to onomati mou. The Father (subject) will send (pempsei G3992 future) the Spirit in the Son's name (en to onomati mou).
G1607 ekporeuomai: depart, proceed, project. NT occurrences include John 15:26 (Spirit proceeds from the Father), Matt 4:4 (word proceeds from God), Rev 22:1 (river proceeds from the throne).
John 14:16: erotoeso ton patera kai allon parakleton dosei hymin. Son asks, Father gives (didomi G1325) another Helper (allos parakletos).
Greek of John 15:26: Hotan elthe ho parakletos hon ego pempso hymin para tou patros, to pneuma tes aletheias ho para tou patros ekporeuetai, ekeinos martyresei peri emou.
Methodist Article IV: The Holy Ghost, proceeding from the Father and the Son, is of one substance, majesty, and glory with the Father and the Son, very and eternal God.
Orthodox Church in America: He proceeds from the Father in eternity in a divinely instantaneous and perpetual movement; the filioque is rejected as unscriptural.
Augsburg Confession I: three Persons, of the same essence and power, who also are coeternal, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost.
